contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Use binary search only when midpoint evidence proves that one complete side cannot contain the answer; otherwise retain candidate-by-candidate scanning.

items.prompt: In ascending data, nums[mid] is smaller than target. What makes discarding every index at or left of mid legal?

options.text: Sorted order proves every value at or left of mid is also smaller than target.
Mid is near the center, so the left side is probably wrong.
The code uses left, right, and mid.
A linear scan would eventually pass mid.

feedback.reason: A legal half discard is a logical implication from the midpoint observation and domain order.
feedback.details: The entire left region is eliminated because its values are bounded above by nums[mid], not because it contains roughly half the indices.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_position: Probability is not a correctness proof.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.variable_names: Names do not establish regional value relationships.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_equivalence: The scan behavior does not justify skipping unchecked candidates.

items.prompt: An unsorted array has nums[mid] smaller than target. What can be concluded about values to the left of mid?

options.text: Nothing directional; the target may appear anywhere, so scanning remains necessary.
Every left value is smaller than target.
The left half can be discarded because mid is too small.
The right half can be discarded because mid is too small.

feedback.reason: A local comparison without global order does not eliminate a region.
feedback.details: Linear scan remains correct because every unchecked position is still a candidate.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_smaller: Unsorted positions have no such ordering guarantee.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.discard_left: The target may be in the discarded half.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.discard_right: The target may also be in the right half.

items.prompt: A predicate over candidates is false for small values and true for all larger values. P(mid) is false. Which discard is legal in a first-true search?

options.text: Discard mid and every smaller candidate.
Discard every candidate greater than mid.
Keep mid and search only smaller values.
Return mid as the first true.

feedback.reason: Monotonic false-to-true structure proves the entire prefix through mid is false.
feedback.details: This is the predicate analogue of eliminating one sorted-array half.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.discard_right: The first true must lie to the right when mid is false.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.keep_mid_left: Mid is known false and smaller values are also false.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.return_mid: The predicate is false at mid.

items.prompt: A condition over sorted values follows false, true, false. The midpoint condition is true. Which conclusion is valid?

options.text: No complete side can be discarded solely from that truth value because the condition is nonmonotonic.
Discard the right side because true has already been found.
Discard the left side because mid is true.
The search is logarithmic because a midpoint was evaluated.

feedback.reason: Binary search needs an implication covering a whole side, not merely a useful midpoint value.
feedback.details: An arbitrary condition must be scanned or handled with a different structural argument.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.discard_right: The condition becomes false and may change again; the desired contract is not specified as a boundary.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.discard_left: Earlier or later true values may exist without one directional transition.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.logarithmic: Evaluation at mid does not guarantee half elimination.

items.prompt: In exact binary search, nums[mid] is greater than target. Why is right = mid - 1 stronger than right = mid for a closed interval?

options.text: Mid is proven not to match, so removing it guarantees progress while preserving every possible target.
Mid may still equal target.
The two updates are always equivalent style choices.
Linear scan requires mid - 1.

feedback.reason: A legal discard should remove all proven-impossible candidates, including mid after a strict mismatch.
feedback.details: Boundary updates must encode both the semantic proof and strict interval shrinkage.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_may_match: The strict greater-than comparison has ruled that out.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.style_only: Keeping a known-wrong midpoint can stall or alter the interval contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_requires: The update follows binary-search correctness and progress, not scan mechanics.

items.prompt: A rotated sorted array with distinct values has an ordered left half. The target lies outside that half’s endpoint range. Which reasoning justifies discarding it?

options.text: Because the half is ordered, every value in it lies within its endpoints, and target lies outside that range.
The pivot is probably on the other side.
The midpoint variable identifies the correct half automatically.
Rotated search always discards the left half.

feedback.reason: Even in rotated search, elimination depends on a region whose values are known to occupy a specific range.
feedback.details: The ordered-half test supplies the missing implication that a scan does not require.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.rotation_guess: Probability is insufficient without the ordered-range proof.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_name: The half must be classified and compared with target.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_right: The retained side depends on order and target range.

items.prompt: A proposed search checks the midpoint of a sorted array for whether the value is prime and discards the left half when it is not prime. What is wrong?

options.text: Primality is not monotonic in sorted numeric order, so a nonprime midpoint says nothing about one whole half.
Sorted arrays cannot contain numeric values.
Prime values always lie to the right.
The only issue is that primality testing has nonconstant cost.

feedback.reason: Sorted values help only when the query predicate respects that order.
feedback.details: A scan is required to find a prime unless another structure or preprocessed index is introduced.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_not_numeric: They can; the predicate is the issue.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_too_expensive: Even a constant-time primality oracle would not provide a half-discard rule.

items.prompt: A first-true search uses a half-open interval [left, right). P(mid) is true. Why is right = mid legal while returning mid immediately may be wrong?

options.text: Mid is a valid candidate, but an earlier true candidate may still exist.
Mid is known false.
The first true must be to the right of mid.
Boundary search can never return a first true value.

feedback.reason: Boundary search may retain a midpoint rather than accept it as final.
feedback.details: The discard rule removes candidates strictly right of mid while preserving the possibility of an earlier transition.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_false: The predicate is true at mid.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.right_side_required: A true midpoint bounds the first true at or left of mid.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_only: It can when the invariant is preserved.

items.prompt: Which observation distinguishes a legal binary-search iteration from merely checking a middle element during a scan?

options.text: The result at mid establishes a regional implication that eliminates candidates without inspecting them.
The inspected position is numerically near n/2.
Two integer variables are maintained.
Only one comparison is made in the iteration.

feedback.reason: Binary search is defined by justified candidate elimination.
feedback.details: Midpoint sampling without a side-wide implication remains an arbitrary inspection order.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.middle_position: A scan can inspect the middle first without gaining elimination power.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.two_boundaries: Variables alone do not justify exclusion.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_comparison: The meaning of the comparison, not its count, is decisive.

items.prompt: Which facts can justify discarding one side of a search domain? Select all that apply.

options.text: A strict comparison in data sorted by the same key as the query.
A proven one-transition predicate over ordered candidates.
An identified ordered half plus target-range exclusion in a distinct-value rotated array.
The candidate is near the numerical center.

feedback.reason: A legal discard is supported by a domain-wide implication.
feedback.details: Geometric centrality is useful only because it balances a discard already proven correct.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_is_center: Central position alone says nothing about answer location.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_comparison: Order extends the midpoint comparison to a whole side.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.ordered_rotated_half: The local ordered range supports elimination.

items.prompt: Which situations do not provide a legal half-discard rule? Select all that apply.

options.text: Exact target search in arbitrary unsorted data without preprocessing.
A condition that alternates true and false across sorted values.
First match in original order when only a key-sorted copy is available without provenance.
First value greater than or equal to target in ascending data.

feedback.reason: Scanning remains necessary when unchecked candidates cannot be excluded in groups.
feedback.details: Lower bound is the contrasting case because its predicate partitions the array monotonically.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.lower_bound: This has a monotonic value >= target predicate.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.original_first_without_index: Key order does not order the required identity.

items.prompt: Which properties must a binary-search update satisfy after midpoint evaluation? Select all that apply.

options.text: Retain every candidate still capable of satisfying the result contract.
Remove only candidates justified as impossible by the invariant.
Strictly reduce the active candidate set on every nonterminal branch.
Remove exactly half on every input.

feedback.reason: Correct binary-search updates combine preservation, proof, and progress.
feedback.details: The asymptotic benefit comes from proportional shrinkage, but the semantic requirements apply first.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.remove_half_exactly: Intervals may differ by one and duplicate ambiguity can reduce progress; correctness does not require an exact half count.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.strict_progress: Termination and logarithmic iteration count require progress.

items.prompt: Which statements correctly contrast scanning with binary elimination? Select all that apply.

options.text: A scan remains correct by checking candidates individually when no regional implication exists.
Binary search skips candidates only after proving they cannot contain the answer.
A scan may still stop early after finding a result that satisfies its output contract.
Any algorithm that checks mid is binary search.

feedback.reason: The methods differ in how they justify leaving candidates uninspected.
feedback.details: A scan checks or reaches them sequentially; binary search excludes them by invariant.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_by_mid: Midpoint inspection without elimination is not binary search.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.scan_checks_candidates: It does not need a discard proof.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.scan_can_early_exit: Linear worst case does not forbid early termination.

items.prompt: Arrange the reasoning for discarding the left side in ascending exact search when nums[mid] < target.

elements.text: Establish nums[mid] is too small.
Use ascending order to show every index at or left of mid is no larger than nums[mid].
Conclude none of those indices can contain target.
Set left = mid + 1 and continue.

feedback.reason: The boundary update is the final consequence of a regional proof.
feedback.details: Skipping directly from comparison to assignment can hide an invalid assumption when the query changes.

items.prompt: Arrange the reasoning for a first-true false-to-true predicate when P(mid) is true.

elements.text: Establish that mid is a true candidate.
Use monotonicity to show every larger candidate is also true.
Conclude the first true lies at mid or earlier.
Retain the left region including mid and discard candidates strictly right of mid.

feedback.reason: A true result does not finish a first-true search; it changes which region remains relevant.
feedback.details: The transfer boundary is the exact answer contract: any true could return, first true must continue left.

items.prompt: Arrange the audit of a proposed half-discard condition.

elements.text: State the ordered candidate domain and exact desired result.
List every possible midpoint outcome.
For each nonterminal outcome, prove which whole region is impossible.
Verify the code removes only that region and strictly shrinks the interval.

feedback.reason: A discard rule is reviewed as a proof table, not as a memorized code pattern.
feedback.details: If any branch lacks a side-wide implication, scanning or another strategy is required.

items.prompt: Arrange the fallback reasoning when a proposed condition is not monotonic.

elements.text: Exhibit candidates a < b < c where the condition changes direction more than once.
Show that a midpoint truth value cannot classify one entire side.
Reject the proposed half-discard update.
Use a scan, preprocessing index, or another structure that matches the condition.

feedback.reason: A concrete counterexample is enough to invalidate the universal binary-search implication.
feedback.details: The alternative strategy depends on workload and whether useful preprocessing can create a monotonic representation.

items.prompt: Arrange the two-candidate progress check for a branch that retains mid.

elements.text: Assume right = left + 1.
Compute which boundary the chosen midpoint formula returns.
Apply the branch update that retains mid.
Confirm the resulting interval is strictly smaller; otherwise change midpoint bias or update.

feedback.reason: The smallest nontrivial interval exposes non-progress immediately.
feedback.details: A legal semantic discard still needs a mechanical update that terminates.

items.prompt: A legal binary search reduces an n-candidate random-access interval to at most half each iteration, while a scan may inspect every candidate. Select worst-case candidate inspections for both.

feedback.reason: The complexity contrast is earned by the legal discard rule.
feedback.details: Without that rule, the O(log n) count is not available even if the code chooses middle indices.

items.prompt: A loop computes mid but updates left = left + 1 or right = right - 1, discarding only one candidate per iteration. A scan also advances one candidate at a time. Select the worst-case time of both under O(1) checks.
constraints: The interval can shrink by only one position per iteration.

feedback.reason: Using a midpoint does not make the loop logarithmic when progress is linear.
feedback.details: The number of candidates removed per iteration, together with check cost, determines the asymptotic bound.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: Use binary search only when midpoint evidence proves that one complete side cannot contain the answer; otherwise retain candidate-by-candidate scanning.
